spec.reasoning: Three safe perturbation strategies are applied: (1) Lexical: All column headers across both sheets are renamed to semantically equivalent alternatives (e.g., 'wtype_id' → 'worktype_id', 'name' → 'label', 'status' → 'state', 'filter' → 'filter_expr', and camelCase output headers to snake_case equivalents). Sheet names are also renamed ('Input' → 'SourceData', 'Output' → 'ResultData'). The instruction references 'wtype_id', 'name', and 'status' as column-level references and 'filter' as a column reference, so renaming them tests whether the model can adapt. The words 'name' and 'status' appear in the instruction but purely as column references ("at a wtype_id, name, and status level" and "filter data from a column"), so renaming is appropriate. (2) Positional title row insert: A title row is inserted at row 1 on both sheets, pushing headers to row 2 and data down by one row. This breaks any hardcoded assumption that row 1 is the header. The answer position shifts from 'Output'!A1:G10 to 'ResultData'!A1:G11 (but since the title row is part of the sheet, the answer range must be updated by the caller). (3) Distractor column: A plausible but irrelevant column 'priority_level' is added to the SourceData sheet to test whether the model incorrectly tries to use it. No data values match header names, so no data_value_renames are needed.
